one_line_takeaway: A simple fine-tuned ResNet-50 hits AUC 0.9931 / F1 0.9715 detecting fake face portraits on a curated DFFD subset, but the strong number is in-distribution only, with no real baseline, no cross-generator test, and no adversarial evaluation.
key_finding: The adapted ResNet-50 reaches AUC = 0.9931 and F1 = 0.9715 on the held-out DFFD subset (precision 0.9815, recall 0.9616, accuracy 0.9611 at the ideal threshold 0.6587), misclassifying only 3.89% of test images (fp=234, fn=496 out of 18,744). A secondary methodological finding from the authors' own ablation: fine-tuning the entire network in Step 1 captured essentially all the performance; the subsequent partial-fine-tuning Steps 2 and 3 added negligible or zero validation-loss improvement and 'could have been skipped.' They also observe the per-class KDE curves intersect very close to the ROC-derived ideal threshold, and note the model is over-confident (predictions cluster near 0 or 1 even when wrong).
core_contribution: A student/lab paper (OTH Regensburg) presenting a deliberately simple binary deepfake-detector for still face-portrait images. The authors take a pre-trained ResNet-50 (ImageNet weights), replace its 1000-way average-pool + FC + softmax head with a flatten + 1-neuron dense layer + sigmoid, resize inputs to 299x299, and train it via a three-step transfer-learning/fine-tuning schedule to output a single real-vs-fake probability. Their own contribution is (1) this adapted ResNet-50 model, (2) a hand-curated subset of the Diverse Fake Face Dataset (DFFD) with manual quality/alignment filtering and video-frame leakage removal, and (3) a ROC-based ideal-threshold selection plus a KDE/probability-density analysis of the per-class prediction distributions. The XceptionNet (99.26% accuracy) and Capsule-Network (100% AUC) results discussed in the introduction are cited RELATED WORK (Roessler et al. FaceForensics++, Tolosana et al.), NOT this paper's own results; the authors explicitly position their model as a simpler, less compute-intensive alternative to those.
threat_model: Defensive detection against deepfake / synthetically-generated or manipulated face imagery used for disinformation, fake news, and impersonation of politicians and public figures (framing drawn directly from the paper's introduction). The system is a passive forensic classifier: given a single face-portrait image, output the probability it is fake. The paper does NOT consider an adaptive adversary, evasion/anti-forensic attacks, adversarial perturbations, or generalization to generators unseen at training time; robustness is only measured in-distribution on held-out DFFD data. So the effective threat model is limited to detecting known, non-adaptive manipulation families.
limitations: No head-to-head baseline on their own data split: the 99.26% XceptionNet and 100% Capsule-Network figures are quoted from other papers on different datasets, so the claimed 0.99 AUC is not directly comparable and no baseline model was actually re-run here. All evaluation is in-distribution on one curated DFFD subset; there is NO cross-dataset or cross-generator (unseen-manipulation) generalization test, which is the main way deepfake detectors are known to fail. No adversarial-robustness or anti-forensic evaluation despite the disinformation threat framing implying adaptive adversaries. The authors deliberately excluded the harder/lower-quality manipulation sets (StarGAN, Faceswap) and DeepFaceLab (not free), plus manually deselected images, which likely biases reported performance optimistically. Test set is imbalanced ~2:1 fake:real, so accuracy is unreliable (the authors acknowledge this) and threshold selection is tuned to that specific balance. Steps 2-3 of the training pipeline are, by the authors' own admission, superfluous, indicating the described three-step schedule is not a validated contribution. No model interpretability / explanation of which facial regions drive predictions (flagged by the authors as future work). Scope limited to still images (video frames sampled at 1 fps); no temporal/video-level detection.

datasets_used: Diverse Fake Face Dataset (DFFD) - curated subset, 145,533 images total used (105,699 train / 21,090 val / 18,744 test)
Real sources: CelebA (19,923), FaceForensics++ (32,569 collected; 4,753 after frame-leakage filtering), FFHQ (19,999)

Fake sources: Deepfake (32,454 collected; 4,514 used), Face2Face (32,682 collected; 4,634 used), FaceApp (11,809), PG-GAN set 1 (19,943), PG-GAN set 2 (19,962), StyleGAN-CelebA (20,000), StyleGAN-FFHQ (19,996)
Excluded: DeepFaceLab (not free), Faceswap and StarGAN (deemed too low quality)
ImageNet (source of pre-trained ResNet-50 weights for transfer learning)
quantitative_results: Confusion matrix at ideal threshold: tp=12,425, tn=5,589, fp=234, fn=496; overall error rate 3.89%
Trainable parameters by stage: Step1=23,739,393; Step2=14,656,001; Step3=3,621,377
Sensitivity to threshold small: F1 ranges 0.9684-0.9734 across ideal_th +/- 0.1
baselines_compared: No baseline model was re-trained/re-run by the authors on their own data.

Cited-only references (related work, NOT this paper's experiments): XceptionNet on FaceForensics++ (Roessler et al.) reported up to 99.26% detection accuracy; Xception and Capsule-Network in Tolosana et al. reported up to ~100% AUC using whole-face and partial-face (eyes-region) clues respectively.
